The Creation of the World.title: How the Ancient Greeks Explained the World
content: The Greek creation story wasn't written in one place or told the same way by everyone. Different cities and poets had different versions, but they all started with chaos and ended with order - the gods organizing the universe.

The Greeks didn't have modern science, but they were brilliant observers. They noticed patterns: seasons repeat, stars move in predictable ways, rivers always flow downhill. Their myths were attempts to explain WHY these patterns existed.

Mount Olympus is a real mountain - the tallest in Greece at 9,573 feet. Ancient Greeks believed their gods literally lived at the top, above the clouds where humans couldn't reach. Climbers today can hike to the summit in about 2 days.

Greek mythology wasn't just stories - it was their religion, their science, and their moral education all mixed together. Children learned how to behave by hearing what happened to people who angered the gods or broke society's rules.
The Creation of the World.lesson: Greek myths weren't random fantasies. They were the ancient Greeks' way of making sense of a complex, sometimes terrifying world. When we study their myths, we're learning how they thought, what they valued, and what questions they asked about life.

The Twelve Olympians.title: Greek Temples: Houses for the Gods
content: The Parthenon in Athens, built around 447 BC, was dedicated to Athena. It's one of the most famous buildings in history - and it's still standing after 2,500 years! The entire structure was built without mortar (the stuff that glues bricks together) - just precisely cut marble blocks stacked perfectly.

Greeks didn't worship inside temples. The temple was the god's house, where their statue lived. Worshippers gathered outside for prayers, sacrifices, and festivals. Only priests entered the inner chamber.

Each major city had a patron god who protected it. Athens had Athena. Corinth had Aphrodite. Delphi belonged to Apollo. Cities competed to build the most impressive temples to honor their patron and show their wealth.

Archaeological excavations have found thousands of offerings left at temples: coins, jewelry, weapons, pottery, even toys. People brought gifts to ask the gods for help or to thank them for answered prayers.
The Twelve Olympians.lesson: Greek religion was practical and transactional: you gave offerings, performed rituals correctly, and hoped the gods would help you. The ruins of temples all over Greece remind us that religion was a huge part of daily life for ancient Greeks.

Prometheus and the Gift of Fire.title: How Did Ancient Greeks Actually Make Fire?
content: Before matches were invented (1826!) or lighters (1823!), making fire required skill and patience. Ancient Greeks used two main methods: the fire drill (spinning a stick against wood until friction created an ember) and striking flint against iron pyrite to create sparks.

Fire was sacred and precious. Greek homes kept their hearth fire burning constantly because starting a new fire from scratch took serious effort. The goddess Hestia protected the home's hearth - losing your fire was considered bad luck.

In Greek cities, a sacred fire burned in a public hearth dedicated to Hestia. When Greeks established a new colony (a new city in a distant land), they carried fire from the mother city's hearth to light the new city's sacred flame.

Fire wasn't just for cooking and warmth - it was technology. Greeks used fire to make pottery (ceramics), work metal (blacksmithing), make glass, and even as a weapon (Greek fire was an early form of napalm used in naval warfare).
Prometheus and the Gift of Fire.lesson: The Prometheus myth reflects how important fire actually was to civilization. Control of fire meant cooked food, metalworking, protection from predators, and light after dark - basically everything that separated early humans from animals.
